data.title: Why Intel's stock is on track for a historic surge after earnings
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen and Emily Bary

Intel shares could see their best post-earnings performance on record and finally surpass their 2000 closing high amid a 'CPU renaissance'

Intel is benefiting from a flood of demand for server CPUs.

Intel's latest earnings report justified its recent stock surge - and then some.

The stock is due to see a potentially historic gain in Friday's session, judging by Thursday's after-hours action. Shares of Intel (INTC) were up 18.9% in extended trading, and a regular-session gain above 10.7% would make for their best post-earnings reaction on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

That wouldn't be the only milestone. Intel's after-hours stock move translates to a price of $79.27. The stock needs to finish Friday's session above $74.88 to surpass its record close achieved on Aug. 31, 2000.

Intel's earnings report on Thursday showed that the company is seeing stronger-than-expected momentum thanks to the surging popularity of its server chips. The server performance helped drive a sizable overall revenue beat -$13.6 billion for the first quarter, ahead of the $12.4 billion consensus on FactSet.

Analysts had actually been expecting Intel's revenue to fall in the period as the company previously disclosed that it hadn't adequately prepared for the server boom, but in reality Intel ended up posting 7% sales growth.

Intel Corporate Vice President of Investor Relations John Pitzer told MarketWatch that Intel "did a fantastic job growing supply above our expectations" in the latest quarter. That demand for CPUs continues to outstrip Intel's growing supply is a positive sign that trends are sustainable, he added.

Intel's stock has surged more than 224% in the past year, through Thursday's close, and the first-quarter report was what "the bulls needed to justify" that kind of move, Emarketer analyst Jacob Bourne said in emailed comments.

The company once was seen as a laggard in the artificial-intelligence chip race. But its fortunes have shifted due to trends like inference and agentic AI, which depend heavily on CPUs to run. On the earnings call, management disclosed that the "attach" rate of central processing units to graphics processing units has improved dramatically. For training workloads, the balance was one CPU per seven or eight GPUs. But with inference, the ratio is more like one CPU for every three or four GPUs.

The hot demand for Intel's server CPUs provides the company with "a steadier revenue base that's less bound to the PC cycle," Emarketer's Bourne said.

Evercore ISI analyst Mark Lipacis wrote of a "CPU renaissance" that's lifting all boats in the market.

Intel, for its part, acknowledged that broader industry trends were certainly beneficial but that it has its own advantages as well. Management called out Intel's advanced-packaging technology and foundry operations as differentiators that allow the company to quickly adapt.

There were a multitude of financial highlights. For one, Intel's June-quarter revenue outlook surpassed estimates. The company set guidance at between $13.8 billion and $14.8 billion - far ahead of the $13 billion that analysts tracked by FactSet were looking for.

Intel also sported gross-margin improvement, with a 41% adjusted margin in the period versus 36.7% in the fourth quarter. CFO David Zinsner called margin expansion his "top priority."

Even Intel's PC business was resilient. Analysts projected declining revenue for the broader client-computing segment, but it actually grew 1% to $7.7 billion, above the $7.1 billion consensus view.

Pitzer told MarketWatch that the company expects the PC market to be "relatively healthy" in the first half of this year, though rising prices for memory chips will likely impact demand in the second half of the year.

-Britney Nguyen -Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Amazon-backed X-Energy raises over $1 billion in IPO
data.description:
Nuclear reactor developer X-Energy, which is backed by Amazon , said on Thursday it had raised $1.02 billion in its initial public offering in the United States.

The Rockville, Maryland-based company sold 44.3 million shares in its upsized offering at $23 apiece, above the marketed range of $16 to $19 per share.

The IPO comes as the market regains traction after a brief slowdown in March, when volatility linked to Middle East tensions and a broader technology selloff sidelined several issuers. With markets near record highs and risk appetite improving, companies are moving quickly to tap investor demand.

Nuclear power is drawing renewed interest as electricity needs surge, particularly from hyperscalers running energy-intensive AI and cloud infrastructure.

X-Energy, founded in 2009, develops small modular reactor (SMR) technology and manufactures fuel for advanced nuclear systems.

SMRs are smaller and designed to be more cost-efficient than traditional large-scale reactors, which can take years to build. X-Energy is developing its Xe-100 reactor, which uses helium as a coolant instead of water.

Amazon invested about $500 million in the company in 2024 to support the rollout of its SMR technology, as the tech giant seeks reliable, carbon-free power for its growing AI-driven data center footprint.

X-Energy had previously planned to go public through a merger with a blank-check firm backed by Ares Management in 2023, but later scrapped those plans, citing unfavorable market conditions.

J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, and Moelis are acting as the lead joint book-running managers. X‑Energy will begin trading ​on the Nasdaq under the symbol "XE" on Friday.

data.title: Trump on Spirit Airlines: 'I Think We'd Just Buy It' — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider, Brian Schwartz and Alexander Gladstone

President Trump said that the U.S. government could take over Spirit Airlines, the ailing budget carrier that has been negotiating this week for a federal bailout.

"We're thinking about doing it, helping them out, meaning bailing them out, or buying it. I think we'd just buy it," Trump said, speaking from the Oval Office Thursday. "They have some good aircraft, some good assets, and when the price of oil goes down, we'll sell it for a profit."

"I'd love to be able to save those jobs," Trump said. "I'd love to be able to save an airline."

Spirit said it is grateful for the president's support, and looked forward to finding a solution that protects the jobs of its employees and ensures competition and affordable airfare.

The Trump administration this week has been considering a rescue of Florida-based Spirit, which helped pioneer the ultralow cost model. Senior officials have been considering a loan of as much as $500 million, in return for warrants that could give the government a significant stake in the airline, The Wall Street Journal has reported.

Spirit has been laboring to emerge from its second trip to bankruptcy in less than a year. The escalating cost of jet fuel, driven sharply higher by the Iran war, has upended its plan. Creditors in recent weeks have been considering options including a potential liquidation.

A key bondholder group on Thursday voiced opposition to the government bailout plan, concerned it could harm their economic interests and leave them with a small minority stake in the airline.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has been the main proponent of the Spirit deal, seeing it potentially delivering a win for Trump by preserving the airline's roughly 14,000 jobs, the Journal reported this week.

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy has voiced caution, pointing out that past government rescues, like the federal bailout of banks during the 2008 financial crisis, were politically unpopular.

On Thursday, more allies of the administration aired reservations. Stephen Moore, a longtime economic policy adviser to Trump, told The Wall Street Journal that he relayed his concerns to senior White House and administration officials.

"How can one broke government bailout a broke airline? Trump should 'just say no' to a taxpayer bailout!," Moore wrote on X on Thursday. He said he told the Trump administration the same directly.

On Thursday, Trump signaled his interest in going through with a deal. "I like having a lot of airlines so it's competitive," he said. "If we could get it for the right price, I'd do it."

Trump said he had a candidate in mind to run Spirit.

Write to Alison Sider at alison.sider@wsj.com

data.title: Here's how much money the 2026 NFL draft picks will make - and who's at risk of losing millions
data.description:
By Weston Blasi

Three NFL players will make over $50 million in tonight's draft

Fernando Mendoza of the Indiana Hoosiers speaks to the media during the 2026 NFL draft combine.

During the NFL's 2026 draft on Thursday night, 32 of the best college football players will join the league.

The NFL draft is a celebration of all the hard work that these players have put in to achieve their dreams. And while a lot of dreams are coming true tonight, there's a lot of money at stake, too.

The earlier a player is selected, the more money he stands to make, thanks to the NFL's rookie wage scale. This system dictates that fixed salaries are tied to specific draft positions, meaning a higher draft pick results in a more lucrative overall contract and a larger signing bonus. Being selected early isn't just a matter of prestige - it's also a major financial advantage.

First-round picks in the NFL draft will receive a four-year contract with a team option for a fifth year, as well as a signing bonus. The salary and signing bonus are fully guaranteed (paid out regardless of injury, performance or release) while other elements of the contract, like roster bonuses, are not.

While Indiana's Fernando Mendoza is expected to be the first pick, the rest of the draft is wide open. But one thing is guaranteed: The top three picks in the draft will each make $50 million overnight.

Wages for this draft class are far from NFL records, as the league introduced a rookie wage scale in 2011 to keep salaries for its youngest players lower. Former Rams quarterback Sam Bradford signed a six-year, $78 million contract as a rookie in 2011, the last year before the rookie wage scale was implemented.

To understand just how much money is at stake tonight, here are the estimated contracts and first-year salaries for the first-round selections of the 2026 NFL draft, according to contract monitoring site Spotrac.

   Draft pick  Total Value   Year 1 Salary 
   1           $54,565,500   $9,921,000 
   2           $52,103,630   $9,473,387 
   3           $50,537,014   $9,188,548 
   4           $48,746,540   $8,863,007 
   5           $45,613,238   $8,293,316 
   6           $40,018,084   $7,276,015 
   7           $35,541,950   $6,462,173 
   8           $31,065,822   $5,648,331 
   9           $30,841,760   $5,607,593 
   10          $29,611,072   $5,383,831 
   11          $27,708,728   $5,037,950 
   12          $25,023,034   $4,549,642 
   13          $24,351,620   $4,427,567 
   14          $23,232,590   $4,224,107 
   15          $22,785,000   $4,142,727 
   16          $21,442,128   $3,898,569 
   17          $20,994,516   $3,817,185 
   18          $20,435,008   $3,715,456 
   19          $20,099,288   $3,654,416 
   20          $19,987,396   $3,634,072 
   21          $19,875,486   $3,613,725 
   22          $19,651,672   $3,573,031 
   23          $19,427,870   $3,532,340 
   24          $18,980,258   $3,450,956 
   25          $18,756,456   $3,410,265 
   26          $18,532,620   $3,369,567 
   27          $18,308,844   $3,328,881 
   28          $18,196,940   $3,308,534 
   29          $17,298,970   $3,145,267 
   30          $16,823,440   $3,058,807 
   31          $16,425,104   $2,986,382 
   32          $16,168,614   $2,939,748

So if a player gets picked a few slots lower than expected, he could lose out on a few million dollars over the life of his rookie contract. Drop 10 spots, and a player could lose out on tens of millions of dollars. Fall out of the first round completely, and a player could lose out on nearly $50 million in total guarantees. And a catastrophic draft-day fall is not uncommon. In fact it happens almost every year.

No player epitomizes the fragility of NFL draft day quite like former University of Colorado quarterback, and now Cleveland Browns player, Shedeur Sanders.

Sanders, the son of NFL legend Deion Sanders, was once projected to go as high as No.1 overall in the 2025 NFL draft, with No. 3 overall to the New York Giants seemingly as low as he could go, according to betting odds from nearly every sportsbook. His long-rumored destination at pick No. 3 to the Giants would have paid him a total guaranteed contract of $39.9 million. But in a draft-day surprise, he was eventually taken in the fifth round - at No. 144.

Sanders lost out on roughly $40 million in total wages from where he was projected to be drafted, and signed a deal totaling $4.6 million over his first four years.

The quarterback position is one of the most difficult to scout, as well as one of the most coveted in the sport, so it's often quarterbacks whose draft positions can be the most volatile on draft night.

That's not the only financial wrinkle to the 2026 NFL draft, however.

The draft comes at an inflection point for college sports, and particularly college football. Due to the introduction of name, image and likeness (NIL) rules in college sports, some top college football players are currently getting paid millions of dollars a year. University of Texas quarterback Arch Manning has an NIL valuation of $5.4 million, for example, and Ohio State wide receiver Jeremiah Smith has an NIL value of $4.2 million, according to database On3.

Former University of Miami quarterback Carson Beck has entered the draft, but he isn't expected to be a first-round selection. If he is selected late in the draft, there's a chance he may be one of the players taking a big pay cut from his college days. Beck received $4 million for his final season at Miami, CBS Sports has reported.

There's been a trend in recent years of some top college athletes making less once they turn pro. It has happened in football and also women's basketball.

"They could make more in college, actually," Lisa Delpy Neirotti, the director of the sports-management program at George Washington University's business school, told MarketWatch. "You're seeing [college] players get $4 million. Maybe even more."

In addition, President Donald Trump on Tuesday repeated his call for Congress to pass a law that would rein in college sports and put limits on player movement and wages.

The first round of the 2026 NFL draft will be broadcast on NFL Network, ESPN and ABC beginning at 8 p.m. Eastern. Rounds 2-7 will take place from Friday to Saturday on those same channels.

-Weston Blasi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: JGBs Fall, Tracking Declines in U.S. Treasurys — Market Talk
data.description: JGBs fall in early Tokyo trade, tracking overnight price declines in U.S. Treasurys. Both JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. JGB prices may also be weighed by data released earlier that showed Japan's consumer inflation rose at a slightly quicker pace in March as the impact of the Middle East conflict boosted energy costs. "With underlying inflation remaining firm, there continues to be a strong case for the Bank of Japan to resume its tightening cycle before long," says Abhijit Surya, senior APAC economist at Capital Economics, in commentary. The two-year JGB yield edges 0.5 bp higher to 1.355%; the 10-year yield is up 2 bps at 2.440%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Nikkei Rises 0.6%, Led by Chip Stocks — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks are higher, led by chip-related shares, despite continued uncertainty over the U.S.-Iran conflict. Lasertec is up 4.0%, and Rohm is 3.6% higher. Investors are closely watching developments in the Middle East as well as Japanese corporate earnings. Fanuc is set to announce its quarterly results later Friday. The dollar is at 159.76 yen, compared with Y159.60 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.6% at 59502.77. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: Tesla Eyes 20% Production Surge In Europe On Strong EV Momentum
data.description:
Key points:
  • Thierig said that the company will hire about 1000 new employees to enable the scale in production starting in May and convert several hundred temporary employees to permanent positions.
  • According to data from the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association, 78,336 Tesla vehicles were registered in Europe in the first quarter. 
  • TSLA shares closed down 4% on Thursday, on the heels of the company providing increased capex guidance of over $25 billion for fiscal 2026.

EV giant Tesla Inc (TSLA) is looking to scale production of its best-selling Model Y vehicle at its gigafactory in Berlin, Europe, according to executive André Thierig.

Thierig, Senior Director of Manufacturing at the gigafactory, said in a post on X that production increase will commence from the second half of the year. He also said that the company will hire about 1,000 new employees to enable the scale in production starting in May and convert several hundred temporary employees to permanent positions.

“This follows a record first quarter 2026 where we built more than 61,000 MY!,” the executive said.https://x.com/AndrThie/status/2047354076556390845?s=20

Tesla Sales In Europe

According to data from the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association, 78,336 Tesla vehicles were registered in Europe, including the UK and the European Free Trade Association (EFTA) region, in the first quarter. This marks a growth of about 45% year-over-year and is largely due to growing demand for battery electric vehicles in the region.

Giga Berlin primarily ships Model Y vehicles throughout Europe, including countries like Belgium, Denmark, France, Italy, Norway, Spain, Sweden, and the UK. It also manufactures vehicles to be shipped to India.

According to Tesla’s latest quarterly report released earlier this week, the factory in Berlin currently has an installed annual manufacturing capacity of over 375,000 Model Y vehicles, implying significant capacity to scale.

Increased Production Outlook

“We plan to keep growing volumes further, not just in Berlin, but across all our factories. Our biggest limiter continues to be our battery pack capacity, and we are actively working on resolving that,” Tesla CFO Vaibhav Taneja said on Wednesday during Tesla’s first quarter earnings call, while also acknowledging a “demand surge” in Europe.

TSLA shares closed down 4% on Thursday, on the heels of the company providing disappointing future guidance, including a fiscal year 2026 capex estimate of over $25 billion, which concerned investors despite Q1 earnings beating Wall Street estimates.

How Did Retail Traders React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around TSLA stayed within the ‘extremely bullish’ territory over the past 24 hours, while message volume stayed at ‘high’ levels.

A Stocktwits user expressed optimism for the stock going much higher despite the slump after the Q1 report.

https://www.stocktwits.com/_Tommy_/message/651216894

Another highlighted that the increased capex guidance of over $25 billion will be “painful but huge.”

https://www.stocktwits.com/nostoplosss/message/651216210

TSLA stock gained about 49% over the past 12 months

data.title: UK to face 'big tariff' if it doesn't drop tech tax, Trump says
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said the United States will retaliate with a "big tariff" if Britain continues to target companies like Apple , Alphabet's Google and Meta with its technology tax.

The United Kingdom rolled out its 2% digital services tax in 2020, a move that has been criticized by Trump and his predecessor Democrat Joe Biden.

"If they don’t drop the tax, we’ll probably put a big tariff on the UK," the president told reporters during a White House event.

His comments come ahead of a visit by Britain's head of state, King Charles, next week.

Trump said he believes the monarch could help repair the Washington's relationship with the British, which has become strained in recent months.

data.title: Bain Capital Life Sciences Secures Board Role at Kailera Therapeutics with 17.4% Stake
data.description:
BCPE Perseus Investor, LP disclosed a 20.3% stake in Kailera Therapeutics, Inc. common stock. The position reflects pre-IPO preferred shares that converted at the IPO and an additional 8.4 million shares purchased on April 20, 2026. The move gives Bain Capital’s affiliate significant influence alongside its existing board representation.

Investor Intent

BCPE Perseus Investor says it will "take an active role in working with the Issuer's management and the Board on operational, financial and strategic initiatives." Andrew Kaplan, a Partner at Bain Capital Investors, already sits on Kailera Therapeutics’ board, positioning the firm to engage directly on priorities.

The investor may change the size of its holding over time and could buy more shares or sell part of its position as it evaluates the company’s progress. It describes the stake as an investment and plans ongoing reviews of performance and strategy.

Investor's Background

BCPE Perseus Investor, LP is a Delaware limited partnership affiliated with Bain Capital Fund XIV and managed through Bain Capital Investors, LLC. Bain Capital is a global private investment firm known for active ownership across private equity, credit, and healthcare-focused investments. The firm often partners with management teams to drive operating improvements and long-term growth.

Original SEC Filing: Kailera Therapeutics, Inc. [ KLRA ] - SCHEDULE 13D - Apr. 23, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: South Korea Seen Extending Chip-Driven Export Growth in April — Market Talk
data.description: South Korea's exports are likely to extend their strong semiconductor-driven growth in April, Citigroup analysts say. Pointing to record-high chip exports for the first 20 days of April, the analysts expect the country's outbound shipments to rise 46.3% on year this month, following a revised 49.2% in March. They expect imports to rise 12.9% on year in April, resulting in a $24.8 billion trade surplus after a revised $26.24 billion surplus in March. "A historically strong current-account surplus will likely continue on the back of semiconductor exports despite higher energy prices," they say in a note. The trade data for April are due next Friday.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Key facts: Strategy Inc buys 34,164 BTC; BTC nears $80k; Exchanges down
data.description:
• Strategy Inc bought 34,164 BTC for about $2.54B at an average $74,395, raising its holdings to 815,061 BTC valued about $61.56B at an average $75,527.1

  • Traders watch BTCUSD ahead of the weekly candle close; BTC has stayed below the 21-week EMA and 20-week SMA since Oct 2025. Weekly close will show price relative to those moving averages.8
  • BTCUSD formed an ascending triangle; Supertrend flipped red-to-green. RSI and MACD rising, signaling bullish momentum for traders.9
  • Bitcoin briefly dipped below the Short-Term Holder realized price after the Q4 2025 drawdown; recent rallies reached near that level, and current price action could retest it if momentum holds.10
  • Fidelity Investments’ Jurrien Timmer commented that Bitcoin has been building a large base and noted the recent move up from $60,033, while also mentioning a potential bear-flag pattern in that rally.11

  • U.S. CLARITY Act in Senate could pass next month; summary says it may affect Ethereum (ETHUSD), a key trading asset. Traders should watch regulatory risk and potential market impact.3

  • Ethereum (ETHUSD) uses proof-of-stake; a successful attack would need buying and risking billions in ETH and risk stake slashing under network rules.7

data.title: Oil Rises as Strait of Hormuz Remains Closed — Market Talk
data.description: Oil rises in early trade amid the ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump said the Strait remained "sealed up tight" and ordered the U.S. Navy to "shoot and kill" any boat laying mines in the waterway. "The closure of the Strait of Hormuz remains the decisive factor," Commerzbank Research's Barbara Lambrecht says in a research report. As long as there is no end in sight to the blockade of the strait, which is essential for oil trade, prices are likely to remain above USD100 per barrel, the commodity analyst adds. Front-month Brent crude oil futures are up 1.1% at $106.26 per barrel; front-month WTI crude oil futures are 1.0% higher at $96.80 per barrel. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Amazon-backed X-Energy raises $1.02 billion in IPO
data.description: Nuclear reactor developer X-Energy, which is backed by Amazon , said on Thursday it had raised $1.02 billion in its upsized initial public offering in the United States.

data.title: WHO global alliance delivers 100 million childhood vaccine doses since 2023
data.description:
By Christy Santhosh

More than 100 million vaccine doses have been delivered to 18.3 million children worldwide since 2023 under a global drive to reverse pandemic-related declines in childhood immunisation, the World Health Organization and vaccine alliance Gavi said on Thursday.

The organizations had launched "The Big Catch-Up" during the World Immunisation Week in 2023. The initiative, focused on children aged 1 to 5 years and spanning 36 countries, concluded in March this year.

About 12.3 million children who were previously "zero-dose" and had never received a vaccine were immunized against diseases such as diphtheria and polio, the agencies said. Around 15 million children had not received a measles shot before the drive.

While final data is still being compiled, the global initiative is on track to meet its target of reaching at least 21 million un- and under-immunised children, the agencies said.

The push comes at a time when some traditional backers such as the U.S. are scaling back aid even as millions of infants still miss routine immunisation every year, leaving them vulnerable to preventable diseases such as measles, diphtheria and polio.

Ephrem Lemango, Chief of Immunization at UNICEF, said recent sharp funding cuts to global health have "seriously affected delivery of immunization services" and could "likely reverse hard earned progress".

Last year, U.S. Health Secretary and long-time vaccine skeptic Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cut financial support for Gavi, a group that helps buy vaccines for the world's poorest countries. He claimed the group ignores safety issues with the immunisations it provides.
